content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. a single line or scene of dialogue that deserves an award purely for the actor’s delivery, not the writing, not the context, just the way they said it? TYIA

**Score:** 101
**Body:** I am curious as to who you will choose. I choose Anthony Hopkins in The Silence of the Lambs, his very first “Good evening, Clarice.” The way he draws it out is chilling in a way that’s unforgettable. 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. "Game over, man! Game over!"
  - Also “Get away from her, you BITCH” is eligible imho
2. That'll do pig, that'll do.
3. This watch was on your Daddy's wrist when he was shot down over Hanoi. He was captured, put in a Vietnamese prison camp. He knew if the gooks ever saw the watch it'd be confiscated, taken away. The way your Dad looked at it, that watch was your birthright. He'd be damned if any slopes were gonna put their greasy yella hands on his boy's birthright. So he hid it in the one place he knew he could hide something. His ass. Five long years, he wore this watch up his ass. Then he died of dysentery, he gave me the watch. I hid this uncomfortable hunk of metal up my ass two years. Then, after seven years, I was sent home to my family. And now, little man, I give the watch to you.
4. I hated her so... much... it... it... the... it... the... flames... flames... flames... on the side of my face... breathing... breathless... heaving breaths... heaving...
5. "All those moments will be lost in time, like tears in rain."
6. “I didn’t kill my wife!”

TLJ: “**I don’t care!**” 

Totally improvised line, not only did they keep it but the script was adjusted so the characters could refer back to it later.
7. "Shut the fuck up Donnie!"
8. Hello. My name is Inigo Montoya. You killed my father. Prepare to die.
9. Say “what” again, mutherfucker. I dare you.

## 2. What is the funniest movie you have ever watched?

**Score:** 44
**Body:** I just watched Happiness 1998 by Todd Solondz and fucking hell... only movie to have ever given me a panic attack. Been feeling extremely depressed and hopeless since I finished it. I need something to help me feel better, something hopeful and feel-good. I want something that really helped you in a time where you were feeling suicidally depressed. Ideally looking for something light and funny, but I have no triggers and am open to any and all genres so go fucking crazy! Thank you all so very much for you recommendations in advance :) I love you fuckers <3

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Blazing Saddles
2. Young Frankenstein
3. Airplane!
4. Dumb & Dumber
5. **Midnight Run, 95% on RT**, starring Charles Grodin and Robert Dino, is probably the funniest movie I've ever seen. 

https://share.google/nkeuPqvz2QOZfWDzs
6. Tropic thunder
7. What We do in the Shadows
8. Galaxy Quest. 
Spinal Tap.
9. Best In Show.
10. Raising Arizona if you want to belly laugh for 90 minutes

## 3. Looking for a documentary recommendation that shows a realistic look into what it’s like inside American prisons. Coworker doesn’t believe there is anything unsafe or scary about being in an American prison, and is willing to watch any documentary I recommend.

**Score:** 35
**Body:** My co-worker sincerely believes that they’re not dangerous or unsafe AT ALL and that this is just slander invented by Hollywood to “make us look bad” and “sell scary movies”. Looking for something that will show her WHY so many people feel like an American prison is the most unsafe place in America. 


She has “all the streamers” she says, which I imagines includes Netflix Hulu Amazon HBO. It’s preposterously naive. She doesn’t even believe us that they’re are gangs in prison, or violence, or drugs. She crossed her arms and huffed “how could you even do any of that with the guards there?” and really said it from her chest like she had just PROVED her point lol. Seems she was legitimately surprised when we all corrected her, and surprisingly she was open to the idea of being enlightened and said she’d watch any documentary we recommended. 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Check out the 2017 PBS doc, "Rikers: An American Jail" narrated by Bill Moyers.

Another is the Oscar-nominated "13th" released in 2016.
  - “13th” is very good and explains the systemic racism in how the drug war is waged against people of color.
2. Showing her some A&E docs. They did that long running series Lock Up. It used to play all the time, there's lots of episodes.

But, the other recommendations are good, and maybe more to the point you need to make.
3. Solitary, inside Red Onion State Prison. I believe it's a HBO film, but you may be able to find it somewhere else.

The Farm: Angola, USA is another one
4. Louis Theroux: Behind Bars (2008) It’s on BBC iPlayer, if you’re able to access that.  Some clips on YouTube.
5. After watching any of the recommendations below, I invite everyone in this thread to watch the short film "Quilters" on Netflix as a palette cleanser. It's about men in prison sentenced for life starting a quilting charity where they make blankets for foster children and autistic children. It's one of my favorite things I've watched all year.
6. Shot Caller
7. LOCK UP ON MSNBC. It’s a documentary TV show. Each episode they go into a different prison, film, and interview inmates.
8. 60 days in is kinda cheesy but shows what is like in some jails. 
9. 13th (2016)

## 4. Sick of Everything — Need a Movie That Matches the Rage

**Score:** 30
**Body:** Lately I’ve been getting sick of the same everyday routine — bored, irritated, frustrated with situations and even people around me. Feels like I’m stuck in a loop, half-assing things and just falling deeper into that hole.

What I need right now is a movie that gives me that **adrenaline rush, that spark, that push**. Something that hits hard and makes me feel alive again. The last time I felt that was watching *The Batman* — that whole arc of Bruce realising “you have to be more” really got to me.

One more thing: I’d prefer if the movie had a **young male protagonist** — someone I can really connect with in that stage of life.

So, hit me with your best recommendations. Any genre is fine — as long as it’s intense, dark, and makes the blood rush.

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. *Falling Down*
  - No better answer than this.
  - Seconded. The best fit for your post
  - Thanks dude...will surely check it out
2. God bless america is fun to match te frustration with people
3. Taxi driver
4. Fight Club
5. Dinner in America
6. Office Space
7. God bless america with Joel Murray and Tara Lynn barr

## 5. What is the best mystery movie you have seen?

**Score:** 31
**Body:** Movies like The Da Vinci Code and Angels and Demons, I'm looking for movies of that style that give you clues that you have to decipher and that in the end maybe have a plot twist that makes you rethink and want to repeat the movie to capture things that you didn't see, another of the examples that I remember most of that type is Nolan's Memento, could you give me suggestions for that, I feel like I've seen most of the mystery movies without finding what I'm looking for.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Murder mysteries/thrillers are generally better. 

The knives out movies are actually decent. So are the “Girl with the Dragon Tatoo” movies. You can watch the originals or just the English language one - which is still great. 

Clue

Chinatown is the movie I tell people is my “favourite movie” when I’m answering the question a certain way. 

Mystic River 

L.A Confidential

All really good movies, but if you just want twists and turns with clues that are harder to sort: 


The Game

Shutter Island

The Prestige 

The Illusionist



Also, out of curiosity, do people still absolutely flame Dan Brown? Dude used to get mocked non-stop when his books are basically for kids.
2. National treasure is entertaining.
3. Chinatown (1974)

Angel Heart (1987)

The Hot Spot (1990)

The Last Seduction (1994)

Devil in a Blue Dress (1995)

The Usual Suspects (1995)

L.A. Confidential (1997)

The Crimson Rivers (France 2000)

Infernal Affairs (Hong Kong 2002), remade as The Departed (2006)

The director's cut of The Butterfly Effect (2004)

In the Electric Mist (2009)

The original version of The Secret in Their Eyes (Argentina 2009)

The Red Riding trilogy: The Year of Our Lord 1974/1980/1983 (Britain 2009)

Shutter Island (2010)

Marshland (Spain 2014)

Canola (Korea 2016)
4. I would recommend some Hitchcock films. My favorites -

Rear Window

North by Northwest 

Shadow of a Doubt

Charade with Cary Grant and Audrey Hepburn is great too and I've heard it called the Hitchcock movie that Hitchcock didn't make.
5. Prisoners 

Wind River 

The Prestige 

Mystic River 

Parasite 

Arlington Road 

Shawshank Redemption 

Se7en
6. The Talented Mr Ripley
7. Gosford Park
8. The Usual Suspects
9. The Last of Sheila
10. The Prestige is the very best example I can think of.   If you haven’t seen it, it’s a must see, maybe more than once.  

Knives Out was fun

---

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. What are some must-watch intelligent movies?

**Score:** 1612
**Body:** Up In the Air (2009)

George Clooney spends his life travelling from company to company firing people. That’s his skill. The people he fires are mostly competent and dedicated but have made the mistake of becoming superfluous according to some Excel sheet. When their employers don’t have the guts to handle the firing themselves, they outsource the task to the company Clooney works for.

Clooney is proud of his professionalism. He does his job efficiently and, in a relentlessly spooky way, humanely. He handles the heartbreak he inflicts every day with icy calm and « look on the bright side » pseudo-compassion. In a hire-and-fire economy, someone has to do this kind of thing, so Clooney does it.

Out of the blue his employer decides to go digital. Soon the firing will be done remotely via a Skype-like app, a “modernisation” personified by new employee Anna Kendrick. Before the remote firing begins, though, Clooney takes her on a few trips to show her the psychological ropes of destroying an employee’s life.

The movie explores the murky depths of a dispenser of searing pain defending old-style ways of dispensing that pain against new ones. Clooney doesn’t challenge the system generating the pain, only its methods.

Under Clooney’s wing, Kendrick begins to realise how profoundly destructive her job actually is, not only to the fired individuals but to herself. She also realises how inadequate her geeky, techy, solutionist outlook on life is. She only wanted to be oh-so-coolly disruptive, poor darling.

Along the way she discovers how Clooney mitigates or ducks the soullessness of his existence. He has turned airports and efficient travel into a hobby—in fact they’ve come to define him—and gives gimmicky self-help talks in airport lounges to justify himself to himself. The superficiality of his existence is brought home to him when he falls in love with a fellow frequent flyer, who turns out to be unwilling to let him, or their relationship, become anything other than superficial.

Clooney and Kendrick give outstanding performances in this wrenching, unforgettable movie.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Margin Call
2. Michael Clayton - beautiful screenplay, perfect performances, great “how things really work” story
  - Tom Wilkinson is incredible in this
3. Conspiracy is criminally underrated.    It’s about the Wannsee conference.    Depressing film but fascinating.
4. Gattaca
5. The Big Short

Moneyball
6. The big short
7. - Arrival

Basically just a well thought out alien movie and what its like to make first contact. Incredibly cerebral and very well written
8. I thought Catch-22 was very crafty. The 1970 movie by Mike Nichols.
9. Margin Call

## 2. What are some movies where the main villain is not that bad?

**Score:** 1516
**Body:** In the movie “The Rock” the main villain, Marine Corps General Francis “Frank” Hummel, basically just wants veteran’s benefits for his men who have died in combat. However, since his men died during covert operations, the military and the government are unable to acknowledge their participation in these activities and thus no benefits can be awarded.

Hummel goes through great lengths to ensure that no people are killed when he steals VX gas to threaten the city of San Francisco. In the end, we even see that he is unwilling to make use of the weapons, because he’s not “fucking insane.” Mason (Sean Connery) instinctively knows this and is not concerned that Hummel will fire the weapons, even though his daughter is living in San Francisco.

Hummel is probably egotistical and self-righteous, believing that it’s his job to right all the wrongs done to his men, even if it means stealing VX nerve gas and threatening to use those weapons on a city full of innocent civilians. However, he is not a psychopath and won’t actually use the weapons on innocent people, because it would simply be a a bridge too far. As far as movie villains go, he’s not that bad.

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. In Bruges.

You've gotta have a code.
  - “Take that part back about my cunt kids!!”
2. Magneto in a couple of the X-men films. He wanted to prevent extermination of his kind.
3. Top Gun. Ice Man had every right to not trust Maverick.
  - Because every time he went up in the air, he was unsafe.  Ice Man didn't like him because he was dangerous.
4. Animal House.  Dean Wormer just wanted the Deltas to be sober and go to class.
  - Fat, drunk, and stupid is no way to go through life son.
5. Hard to say without spoiling, but Gone Baby Gone. Not the mother, that mother was among the most detestable characters I can remember. The “main antagonist” I was kind of with. Great movie.
6. Jaws
7. Terry Benedict in Ocean's 11.

The guy did literally nothing wrong. He doesn't steal from anybody, he doesn't mistreat his girlfriend, and he's even polite to Danny Ocean at first. The movie even establishes that he works extremely hard at his job which means he deserves his success. Still, the movie ends with him losing his money and his girlfriend and they paint it as if he deserves it. He didn't deserve any of it.

## 3. Which actor’s movie look was straight-up breathtaking?

**Score:** 1193

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Elisha Cuthbert - The Girl Next Door.
2. Rachel Weisz in The Mummy, also Brendan Fraser is the Mummy..
3. Career Opportunities. Jennifer Connelly on that mechanical horse.
  - Jennifer Connelly in *Rocketeer*
4. Elizabeth Hurley in Bedazzled (2000)
5. Grace Kelly in Rear Window 🥰
6. It is remarkable how well she fit that role and look. So many people thought she was actually Spanish!
  - She had God tier looks at that stage. A lot of Welsh people have those dark almost Mediterranean looks believe it or not. Tom Jones is another one.
    - So you’re saying it’s not unusual?
7. Marissa Tomei in, My Cousin Vinny. Ugh what a heart throb every time.

## 4. Movies that turn into something unexpectedly horrifying?

**Score:** 1106
**Body:** I still think about Bone Tomahawk (2015) year after watching it. Your typical sheriff investigating a mystery turns into one of the most horrifying and adrenaline inducing movies I’ve ever seen. 

What other movies turn into something unexpectedly horrifying?

https://filmwaffle.com/post/is-bone-tomahawk-2015-actually-good

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Parasite
2. From Dusk Till Dawn
3. The Menu

Heretic

Apostle

Saltburn

Oldboy

Blink Twice
  - Came in halfway through Saltburn with absolutely no context other than “it’s a gay movie” cause some friends were having a watch party and I’m the token straight that gets invited.

Craziest shit I’ve ever seen.
  - Shudders at Oldboy
4. Barbarian
  - When Justin Long’s character abruptly hangs up on his mother to answer his friend’s call with “what up, f•••••t?”

I could not stop laughing.
    - His character was hilariously despicable through and through lol


Finding a murder basement and immediately breaking out the tape measure, >!pushing the protagonist to The Mother to try to distract her!<, he’s such a piece of shit lol
5. Audition
6. Hancock. Starts well with a great premise, and then turns horrifyingly into something shit.

## 5. Anyone else think Tia Carrere is a drop dead gorgeous actress who didn’t get enough roles in the 90’s?

**Score:** 812
**Body:** Rising Sun, Wayne’s World, True Lies. It looked promising. But after that it’s was all B-movies.

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. We're not worthy! We're not worthy! We're not worthy!
  - Schwing!!!
  - She'd give a dog a bone 🦴
2. It helps if you can act
  - Actors hate this one top tip!
3. Loved her in relic hunter
4. Absolutely, also learned from Waynes World that girl can sing!
5. Still a fox in the new Lilo and stitch movie
  - If she lived in 18th century France she would be known as La Renarde.
    - …and she would have been hunted with only her cunning to protect her.

---

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. What are your thoughts on Deliverance?

**Score:** 22
**Body:** Question, What are your thoughts on Deliverance?

I just rewatched this film and the more I go back to this film, the more I think it's one of the best of the 1970s. Everything about this film is perfect, the pacing, the casting, and the directing. Everyone was on their A game in this.

The film is about 4 businessmen who ventured into the wilderness to canoe the Cahulawassee River before it is damned. Burt Reynolds, Jon Voight, Ned Beatty, Ronny Cox all give great performances and I feel Ned Beatty gave the gutsiest performance and should have been nominated for an Oscar. However, I feel Bill McKinney and Herbert Coward steal the show as The Mountain Men, who, thought in 1 (or in Coward's case, 2 scenes), they really portrayed some sick SOB's who decided to have "fun" when they caught Jon Voight & Ned Beatty's character.

One thing that interested me is that although Burt Reynolds broke out with Deliverance and is a main character, it's really Jon Voight who is the main protagonist and the one who has to saved to group and get them to safety after Burt's character become incapacitated.

Another thing about Deliverance that I like is the feeling of something is wrong and the feeling that the 4 are being watched. I will say, all 4 where really out of their element and that they had no idea what they were getting out. I also like the ambiguity and the uncertainty that is present throughout the film.

Overall, I do love Deliverance and I think it is one of the best films of the 1970s. I also think it is a shame that Burt rarely did a film like Deliverance again until I think the 1990s (Look, I like Burt Reynolds and his films but Deliverance was something else)

So, What are your thoughts on Deliverance?

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I usually don't even read reviews of Deliverance or Requiem for a Dream which are films that are excellent but I found them so upsetting that I don't want to watch them again or even think about them. 

But this is a good review. It's a good film. 

I hated it. 

And like many people my age, Gen X, I almost certainly watched it at far too young and impressionable of an age. I am guessing I was 12 or 13 when I saw it. And it was the stuff of nightmares. Not so much for the violence alone, but for the depravity and the depths of casual evil that we see in the film. At that age I was already well acquainted with the puzzling nastiness of bullies. You find amusement in the suffering of others? What the f--k is wrong with you? And I was also by that time already a fairly experienced outdoors person. So I understood just how very, very far a person can quickly get from any kind of civilization or legal authority. In those cases you have to rely on the ethics and decency of others. And it occurred to me that there are people who have no apparent decency.
  - Same. I've only seen it once and I was in my early teens. I have nothing but a horrible memory of the movie and will never watch it again.
2. Great film. I think about this anecdote all the time -

> The film is infamous for the cost cutting by the studio in an effort to kill it and having the actors perform their own stunts, such as Jon Voight notably climbing the cliff himself. Reynolds requested to have one scene re-shot with himself in a canoe rather than a dummy as it tumbled over a real waterfall. Reynolds recalled his shoulder and head hitting rocks and floating downstream with all of his clothes torn off, then waking up with director Boorman at his bedside. Reynolds asked "How'd it look?" and Boorman said, "It looked like a dummy falling over a waterfall."
3. I love it as well. The rape scene gets all the attention, and it's a great scene, but it's a shame because the whole film is brilliant. If you can get over the one scene, it's a great film to watch over and over again because of the contrast between the serenity of the setting and the suspense, which permeates every scene, even at the beginning and the end when they aren't on the river. Lewis (Burt Reynolds) is such a great character becuase he represents several things at once: he is the mentor figure for Ed (Jon Voight), who helps the city boy grow into a nature man. But he is also the river itself... He a primative macho dude, tied to nature, but he is doomed, a dinosaur. So Jon Voight gets a crash-course indoctrination into the world of kill-or-be-killed, and all for naught because the river (and nature itself) is dead in the water. Once they leave the river he'll be stuck with all of these survival skills and ptsd but with no place to apply them. Then you've got other facets of Ed personified as Ned Beatty (innocence) and Ronny Cox (peace and intellect). Ned is innocence lost, and Ronny Cox is literally drowned and killed and then buried, unceremoniously, at sea.

The film is one of the most 70s of all time, with great suspense and action, on-location photography and "new Hollywood" realism, minimal but memorable soundtrack, an amazing and iconic 70s cast, and also a twisted message about man's assault on nature. We're damming up this river. Is that good or bad? On the one hand you're destroying God's green earth, but on the other hand you're burying all the primordial horror that occurs on and under its waves, and along its banks. Something for you to think about as you drive away from the theater while the boys drive back to the city.

Edit: I'm a little younger than the audience at the time (I was born the year of the film's release!) but I'm sure that there's an intentional Vietnam allegory here as well... Men going into a wild place that they didn't belong. And then for modern audiences there is a strong "rural America vs urban America" theme. Something for everyone in such a relatively straightforward story.
  - The focus on the rape scene is unfortunate because it tends to solidify the idea that the evil here is only living in the woods. But the framing device about the dam and the destruction of this river and its valley and all of these folks, good or bad, being unceremoniously driven from their land and cast adrift gets left out of the equation, and it's certainly possible to argue that this is truly the more profound act of rape which occurs.
    - Absolutely! Those scenes of the machines at the end were deliberate.

Another thing I was thinking about... It's like the river is there for men to go and work out their violence. They leave civilization, go to the river, vilolate and kill each other, then go back to town. But the river isn't there anymore, so where else can the dammed-up violence "flow" but back home?
4. My first R rated film.  I was 13 and my older sister bought me my ticket.  I saw it alone.  Shocking and stunning.  It's a film I revisit:


Voight's turn to "play the game"!  Beatty and that McKinney guy, Jesus.  Renyolds the survivalist.  And Cox the moral and the civilized man.


I've read the book and it is true to it.  I would like to read Dickey's poetry one day

## 2. Chow Yun-Fat Contemporary Films That Are Good?

**Score:** 21
**Body:** I just fell into a micro rabbit hole over on r/interestingasfuck where someone had posted the 10 most popular videos on the Chinese version of YouTube. #5 on there was some wild mashup of a movie with Chow Yun-Fat. I then looked him up on IMDB, and sure enough that mashup came from a film that stars the man himself and is coming out this year. It looks like some kind of Ping-Pong Playa/The Good The Bad and the Weird type spoof movie. 

It made me realize I haven't seen any new films with Chow Yun-Fat in them. I feel like I haven't seen him in anything since the late 90s/early 2000s. He sort of slipped off my radar.

I'm a huge fan of The Killer, A Better Tomorrow, Hard Boiled, basically everything he did with John Woo. 

Anyone got any recs for post 2000s Chow Yun-Fat movies?

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I can’t believe he’s 70. Google rabbit hole tells me he completed a half marathon in 2,5 hours earlier this year.

I am a fan of Hong Kong cinema of the 80s/early 90s. Hard Boiled is absolutely gorgeous to look at in places and completely bonkers.

As for recent films, I’m afraid I have nothing to add but I’m following.
2. Let the Bullets Fly

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=PFoLfRA5ghw

Project Gutenberg

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=qFyY27jar1U

Don't know why the original post was removed.  Anyway these are two of his more recent notable films.
3. If you have only watched CYF's action stuff, I would also suggest looking into his non-action stuff in the 80s and 90s. I don't think people can fully appreciate CYF as an actor by just watching his John Woo stuff. Autumn's Tale, Romancing Stars, and God of Gamblers, for example, are all classics. If you only like his John Woo stuff, then you are asking the wrong question - should just watch John Woo movies.
4. The only post-CTHD movies he did I liked were Curse of the Golden Flower, Let the Bullets Fly, and Last Tycoon. Office isn't great but it's kinda fun to watch him star in a musical. The others are hard to recommend.
5. He’s just one of those guys who was born to be a movie star. Instant screen presence. He also a very good actor when he needs to do drama and elevates every movie he’s in. People talk about Tom Cruise being a great action star but I think you can see how much more depth a great actor like Chow brings to a role when compared to Cruise (who does his thing well enough but within a certain type).  

To actually answer your question I’m in the same boat as you. The last film I’ve seen him in was either Golden Flower or the Pirates movie. Doesn’t appear he’s been in anything much of note since. It’s a shame because he’d be so good as an older actor l think and he’s just 70. Michelle Yeoh just won an Oscar at 60 why not him?
  - Oscar isn't everything.  He's not interested in Hollywood and have been in tons of Chinese movies and remains popular.

## 3. I feel like I personally prefer The Wages of Fear to Sorcerer, in terms of how it deals with its characters and tells the story. I would love to hear other perspectives!

**Score:** 6
**Body:** I know this isn't exactly a hot take, but I was actually expecting to prefer Sorcerer going into it, given my interest in both Roy Scheider, and much of William Friedkin's filmography. However, I felt like The Wages of Fear was a better telling of the story. I also still loved Sorcerer, and am aware that a new director's take on a story should be different, and unique to them. For me, its just a matter of preference.

All the extra stuff in the town in the original film, which might be considered worse pacing by some, did more to flesh out the environment and the characters for me, which is ironic considering the fact that the first 20 minutes of Sorcerer literally shows the characters backstories. While I like that first chunk of Sorcerer, I feel like it was kinda unnecessary. The original film builds the character dynamics, based on the tension created by their disagreements. Luigi's conflict with the fake tough guy feels earned, and the main character's attachement to his fake facade make him unlikeable, but very well-realized. It also leads to the payoff of the where the facade slips later on, which was more fascinating than any of the character moments in Sorcerer.

Additionally, I hated the way that the secondary truck explodes in Sorcerer. The characters open up to each other for the first time in the movie, making it super obvious that the truck was gonna explode. Also, it happens on screen, and quite dramatically, kinda ruining the whole "any bump on the road" paranoia that works so well. While the characters in The Wages of Fear also let their guards down at around the same point before the explosion, the two characters who died in the explosion had already gotten to know each other, making their death more shocking.

Also, while the characters may seem to not care much for each other in both movies, the rare moments of comradery, which are immediately crushed by the weight of the situation, are part of what makes The Wages of Fear so interesting. Their joyous laughter upon exploding the rock (and realizing that Luigi is ok), is almost heart-warming, and gives the impression that these characters are finally almost on the same page. Then, the truck blows up off screen, immediately ending the optimistic outlook.

The nostalgia and yearning for home is also far more pronounced in The Wages of Fear, making the scene where the cowardly man lements, and tries to remember France in his final moments much more powerful. Some may call the ending on the nose, and while that may be true, I don't think necessarily undermines its value for me. The frantic editing, combined with the sheer bitterness of it make it work for me - and I feel like it compliments the themes very well.

  
Lastly, I think the thrills and setpieces overall (not counting the bridge scene in Sorcerer), are a bit more tense. The scene in The Wages of Fear where they have to maintain a certain speed before reaching the paved road is probably my favorite in the film, even though I kinda understand why it was excluded. 

I would love to hear what others think, and would love to know which version you prefer and why.

### Top 2 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. There are some otherwise "ok" movies I love for just one scene or image, like the "show me" scene in Christine.  On an intellectual level I do think the Wages of Sin is the better movie, but I'll never get the image of the trucks struggling to cross that bridge out of my head

## 4. Would you say a film needs to be evaluated within context (historical, cultural, technical, as it relates to other movies etc) or is judging it purely on its own preferable/possible?

**Score:** 0
**Body:** Can someone experience, say, Citizen Kane without harkening to its famous historical context, just seeing it as a film, or are the externalities surrounding it something one needs to be paying attention to? It's usually what people mostly talk about when Citizen Kane gets brought up, and people who find it boring or drab are told to "learn the history behind it".

And moreover, does a movie have to be contextually compared to other movies as it relates to them in the annals of cinema in order to be evaluated or experienced "properly"? Can any movie just be "seen" on its own?

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Do you believe that it makes a difference in analyzing Citizen Kane whether or not you know English?

A film’s context is as important in interpreting a film as its choice of language. In many ways, the cinematic language it uses as well is going to be shaped by its context as well. Ultimately, filmmakers are going to be constrained and enabled by the message they are attempting to send by the cultural barriers and tools that we’ve developed to express those messages.

I always use spoken/written language as an example because it’s obvious, but the next step from there is recognizing the constant evolution of cinematic language as well (especially important with regards to Citizen Kane, IMO). And the factors that shape both, well, are cultural and historical.
2. I would say a reasonable distinction between a casual film watcher and someone who cares about the medium, is someone who cares to know about the context around a film, and the pilars there you illustrate. 

Some of the pillars there are quite broad and encompass the experience of the film and its aesthetics, so are not exactly seperate from the experience of the film “on its own”
3. I'd say it depends on what experience you want.  Do you want to be entertained or do you want to learn something?  The two aren't mutually exclusive, but If you want to learn something about the characters and the period of time it was made, context is everything.  If you just want something to watch before you go to bed, you don't need to understand the original audience's experience.
4. I think Breathless is hugely improved by evaluating it with context of Old Hollywood movies and seeing how strongly it diverges from that. You can definitely watch it and enjoy it on its own, but I also recommend watching older movies like Roman Holiday (which is delightful) to see how unique it was. I'm really hoping the new Richard Linklater movie about the making of that film is actually good at contextualizing that AND that it isn't the most annoying movie ever made
5. I believe context to naturally enhance the movie watching experience, and leads to far more interesting discussion and criticism. Is it possible to judge just on its own? I guess so, but it's definitely not preferable IMO.

Meeting a movie on its own terms, finding what the authors were trying to achieve, what were they inspired by, and how movies at that time and place usually were makes me more immersed in the world of the movie. When I see criticisms of old classics saying X is slow, boring or doesn't adhere to current social paradigms I'm just uninterested in what I see as shallow conclusions to make.
6. I think it depends on the movie and person. For instance I watched Citizen Kane as a teenager at my Grandma’s house and didn’t really have any context behind it other than “greatest movie of all time”. which didn’t really mean much to me at 17. So I just watched it as a “movie” and it was ok. It is a single stand alone film made very early in cinema history. I don’t think it needs to be related to any movies made previously in order to enjoy or not. 

Quentin Tarantino’s movies are highly referential. So for his films I do think it’s kind of impossible to watch them without comparing them to the very movies that inspired them. 

Friday the 13th doesn’t need to be compared or given any real context to be enjoyed. The enjoyment comes from the special effects, characters and set rather than the story.

## 5. caught stealing: lustrous & forgettable

**Score:** 0
**Body:** this is a movie you’ve seen before, but “derivative” feels like entirely the wrong epithet: caught stealing has the energy and grit and indulgence that it ought to have. following a formula is not the same as copying. i can think of a myriad things i disliked about this movie, but none of them detracted from what it did right. plot-wise, few surprises; the heartstrings were plucked somewhat listlessly; but the excellence of form more than compensates for the limpid function of all these things. the violence and choreography is so impactful, the gore and makeup excellently done. the car-crash flashback is my favourite scene: the grazing cow, the slow pan, the glimpse of flesh and bone bursting out of baseball whites — it’s gorgeous and chilling and sad, and the sheer sensory weight of this does all it’s supposed to. henry is more believable because of it. the slew of callbacks, allusions to a maybe, the regret and the shame: this scene is the ideational, interpersonal, and textual linchpin

i think what this movie lacks that its genre predecessors don’t is real interpersonal intrigue, two people simultaneously posturing and calculating — i like training day for that driving dialogue, good time for connie’s sheer perspicacity, L.A. confidential for all the station scenes. caught stealing has none of that real, consequential power play, no scenes you can watch over and over while noticing something new about the characters and their world

is depth a requirement when the surface is so gorgeous? i will probably never think about this movie again but i am very, very glad to have experienced it the once
